bibliographic.citation: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, ‘The Eye and the Heart,’ Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 34, new series 22 (1911): 235–265.
bibliographic.title: The Eye and the Heart

entities.description: The complete article represented by CME00095: a seven-paragraph introduction, Hammond's complete transcription and annotation of the Longleat poem, a manuscript-ending note, and an afterword.
entities.label: Hammond 1911 article and Longleat edition
notes: The complete-volume title page confirms Halle a. S., Max Niemeyer, 1911, Band XXXIV / Neue Folge XXII; Internet Archive serial metadata incorrectly exposes the inherited serial start date 1877.
Printed p. 234 precedes the article and p. 266 begins the next article, confirming the complete boundary.

rights.basis: The article was first published in 1911.
rights.component: 1911 article, transcription, notes, and typography

entities.description: The anonymous Middle English translation/version as an abstract work: 103 eight-line stanzas and 824 lines in ababbcbc rhyme, distinct from Hammond's article and from either surviving witness.
entities.label: The Eye and the Heart
notes: DIMEV records one manuscript witness and one early printed witness.
The English translator is anonymous; Michault Taillevent is author of the French source work, not the English translator.

rights.basis: The work was created and published centuries before modern copyright terms.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous Middle English text
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, photographs, or provider-supplied reproductions.

access.accessMethod: Complete and return Longleat's image and video request form to collections@longleat.co.uk. Request a quote for a complete color digital surrogate and separately identify the poem at ff. 102–119.
access.contact: collections@longleat.co.uk; Longleat House Collections, The Estate Office, Warminster, Wiltshire BA12 7NW, United Kingdom
access.format: JPG digital photographs, scanned microfilm PDF, or quoted multi-image order

localCopies.label: Longleat image and video request form and current fee schedule
notes: The cached file is complete as a three-page form but metadata-only relative to the manuscript.
Current form prices: £30 + VAT for the first digital photograph from one volume and £5 + VAT per subsequent image; multiple-volume requests are quoted; scanned microfilm PDF is £45 + VAT.

The form limits a research request to ten images and requires a separate quote/arrangement for broader delivery; a complete 147-leaf color surrogate is therefore not priced by the simple per-image line alone.
A mediated whole-object request should explicitly include covers/boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and every manuscript leaf.

rights.basis: Longleat's request form says research images may not be copied, further circulated, or reproduced without permission; publication requires a separate written, purpose-specific license.
rights.component: Images or microfilm PDF supplied by Longleat for research
notes: The form's license is non-exclusive, non-perpetual, non-transferable, purpose-specific, and may require deletion of supplied images after use; exact terms must be confirmed for the intended repository/publication use.

access.accessMethod: Arrange an advanced reader appointment with Longleat Collections; manuscript consultation requires a research need, registration, identification, and a letter of introduction.
access.format: Onsite consultation; microfilm expected where available

entities.description: The direct manuscript source of Hammond's English text. The Eye and the Heart occupies ff. 102–119, begins without a heading with a four-line capital, and ends without an explicit.

holding.notes: No repository-owned exact item catalog page was found; Longleat's current access page and image-request form provide the institutional access routes.

entities.label: Longleat House, MS 258
notes: Hammond uses legacy recto/verso notation ‘102a’ and ‘119a’; DIMEV records ff. 102–119. The current exhibition labels rectos as 107r and 108r.
The c. 1475–1500 copying date is not the English translation's exact composition date.
Hammond's statement that the poem was unique in manuscript remains compatible with DIMEV's separate early print witness.

physicalDescription.extent: 147 leaves; the fifth gathering is lost
physicalDescription.format: Modern-bound verse miscellany, gatherings chiefly of eight
notes: Hammond describes almost all of the manuscript as the work of one small, legible, current but inelegant hand, generally writing six stanzas per leaf.
Later running titles, corrections, ownership marks, and printer's annotations occur; ‘Constat John Thynne’ appears on the opening page.
physicalDescription.support: Outer sheet of each gathering vellum and three inner sheets paper

rights.basis: Longleat's request form says research images may not be copied, further circulated, or reproduced without permission; publication requires a separate written, purpose-specific license.
rights.component: Images or microfilm PDF supplied by Longleat for research
notes: The form's license is non-exclusive, non-perpetual, non-transferable, purpose-specific, and may require deletion of supplied images after use; exact terms must be confirmed for the intended repository/publication use.

access.accessMethod: No complete public facsimile was found; consult the Huntington Library using STC 6915 and request the exact copy shelfmark and reproduction options.
access.entityLabel: Wynkyn de Worde print of The Eye and the Heart, STC 6915
access.format: Physical early printed book or mediated images

bibliographic.citation: A lytell treatyse called the dysputacyon or the complaynte of the herte through perced with the lokyng of the eye (London: Wynkyn de Worde, [1516?]), STC 6915.
bibliographic.title: A lytell treatyse called the dysputacyon or the complaynte of the herte through perced with the lokyng of the eye

entities.description: A separate early printed witness, conventionally dated [1516?]. The official Longleat exhibition reproduces opening leaf Ai from the Huntington Library copy.

holding.notes: The exhibition identifies the image source as the Huntington Library but does not give the copy's shelfmark.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde print of The Eye and the Heart, STC 6915
notes: No direct copy relationship from Longleat is asserted. The exhibition states that de Worde's layout does not match the manuscript's printer's marks and suggests that a second, now-lost print may once have existed.

rights.basis: The print dates from the early sixteenth century.
rights.component: Underlying early printed text and typography

access.accessMethod: Open the validated JONAS work record and its manuscript-witness list.
access.entityLabel: Debat du coeur et de l'oeil
access.provider: Institut de recherche et d'histoire des textes (IRHT/CNRS)

entities.description: Michault Taillevent's French love debate, composed c. 1444: 824 octosyllabic lines in 103 eight-line stanzas rhyming ababbcbc. It is the source work translated into Middle English, not necessarily the translator's surviving physical exemplar.
entities.label: Debat du coeur et de l'oeil
notes: JONAS records a broad manuscript tradition; British Library Royal MS 19 A III is one witness, not the sole French witness.

rights.basis: JONAS dates the work c. 1444 and identifies its medieval author.
rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century French text

access.accessMethod: Use the Biblissima aggregation for the exact shelfmark and component range; search the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue for institutional access.
access.entityLabel: British Library, Royal MS 19 A III
notes: Biblissima is not the holding institution; it is used because an exact current British Library item page for this shelfmark was not found.

access.accessMethod: Legacy Digitised Manuscripts URLs now redirect to the British Library's general digitised-manuscripts page, and Royal MS 19 A III was not found in the current public digitisation master list.
access.entityLabel: British Library, Royal MS 19 A III
access.format: No verified public whole-manuscript image object

access.accessMethod: Use the British Library online digitisation order form with shelfmark Royal MS 19 A III and specify complete-object or ff. 29r–41v coverage; request color explicitly because existing surrogates may default to monochrome microfilm.
access.contact: British Library Digitisation Services; licensing@bl.uk for reproduction use
access.entityLabel: British Library, Royal MS 19 A III
access.format: Reference or reproduction-quality digital images; manuscript originals are photographed rather than scanned

rights.basis: British Library Digitisation Services warns that supplied images may carry copyright and directs reproduction users to licensing@bl.uk.
rights.component: British Library reference or reproduction images

entities.description: A French manuscript witness containing Michault Taillevent's Debat du coeur et de l'oeil at ff. 29r–41v. Thomas Wright published this witness in 1841, and Hammond used Wright's text for detailed comparison.

holding.notes: Biblissima supplies the exact component mapping; no current repository-owned exact item page for this shelfmark was located during review.

entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 19 A III
notes: Hammond reports that the French leaves were disordered by binding and gives a stanza sequence for reconstructing reading order.
This is a source for Wright's edition and Hammond's comparison, not an established physical exemplar of the anonymous English translator.

rights.basis: British Library Digitisation Services warns that supplied images may carry copyright and directs reproduction users to licensing@bl.uk.
rights.component: British Library reference or reproduction images

access.accessMethod: Browse Internet Archive or download the complete volume PDF.

workPortion.label: Le Debat du Cuer et de l'Oeil
notes: Complete carrier volume retained; the source work begins on p. 310 and ends on p. 321.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., The Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes, Camden Society, original series 16 (London: J. B. Nichols and Son, 1841), 310–321.
bibliographic.title: The Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes

entities.description: Wright's printing of Le Debat du Cuer et de l'Oeil from the Paris manuscript then designated Bibliothèque du Roi 7388, embedded in a larger Camden Society volume.
entities.label: Thomas Wright's 1841 French edition
notes: The printed French poem begins on p. 310 and ends on p. 321; Hammond's ‘pp. 310 ff.’ is not a claim that the work ends at p. 318.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1841.
rights.component: 1841 edition and transcription

workPortion.label: Le Debat du coeur et de l'oeil
notes: The complete facsimile volume is retained. Exact printed leaf signatures for this portion remain to be reconciled.

bibliographic.citation: Le Jardin de plaisance et fleur de rhétorique, facsimile of the edition printed by Antoine Vérard c. 1501, ed. Eugénie Droz and Arthur Piaget (Paris: Firmin-Didot for the Société des anciens textes français, 1910).
bibliographic.title: Le Jardin de plaisance et fleur de rhétorique

entities.description: The Société des anciens textes français facsimile of Antoine Vérard's circa-1501 anthology, including a correctly ordered printing of the French Debat du coeur et de l'oeil.
entities.label: Le Jardin de plaisance 1910 facsimile
notes: The cached complete-volume PDF contains the French debate on digital PDF pages 122–129; the exact original leaf signatures remain to be reconciled.

rights.basis: The facsimile volume was first published in 1910.
rights.component: 1910 facsimile edition

next_steps: If a request is made, record Longleat's reply, quote, deliverable format, physical coverage, and exact license terms.
Request covers/boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and all 147 manuscript leaves; map The Eye and the Heart separately to ff. 102–119.
Do not commit or redistribute supplied files unless written authorization covers the Git repository and any commercial print-on-demand use.
notes: Longleat's official access page and current image-request form provide the identified request and licensing route; no complete facsimile was consulted for this record.
openQuestions.question: Will Longleat supply a complete color digital facsimile of MS 258, including exterior and endmatter surfaces, and what public-repository or publication reuse will it license?

next_steps: Compare all printer marks throughout a complete MS 258 facsimile with the full STC 6915 layout.
Consult current bibliographical scholarship for evidence of a lost second printing.
Retain Longleat and STC 6915 as parallel witnesses until direct copying evidence emerges.
openQuestions.question: Which printing, if any, was prepared from Longleat MS 258's printer-marked leaves?

next_steps: Ask Huntington reference staff to identify the exact copy reproduced by Longleat and its complete-image availability.
Record any ESTC/USTC copy record and distinguish catalog access from image access.
openQuestions.question: What is the Huntington shelfmark and can a complete scholarly facsimile of STC 6915 be viewed or purchased?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether a legacy or restored complete digital object exists.
If ordering, request the whole manuscript for source context where feasible and map the French work to ff. 29r–41v.
openQuestions.question: Does British Library Royal MS 19 A III have a recoverable complete digital object, or must images be ordered through Digitisation Services?

next_steps: Consult a modern critical edition and stemmatic study of the French and English versions.
Do not promote Royal MS 19 A III or the circa-1501 print from Hammond's comparison layer into the anonymous translator's physical source chain without evidence.
openQuestions.question: What French exemplar or branch underlies the anonymous English translation, and can its translation date and place be narrowed?

next_steps: Request or obtain a complete manuscript facsimile and compare every italic span with the manuscript when available.
Document a sign-by-sign expansion policy and preserve uncertainty rather than treating italics as emphasis.
openQuestions.question: Which Hammond/CME italics map to abbreviation signs, supplied letters, or other features in Longleat MS 258?

reviewNotes: The graph distinguishes the complete Hammond article, the abstract English translation, its manuscript and printed witnesses, the French source work and representative French witness, modern digital encodings, and reproduction objects.

A complete public whole-manuscript facsimile of Longleat MS 258 was not found. The cache preserves every Longleat manuscript still image published on the official exhibition page: eight JPEGs in one auditable bundle plus the ninth PNG detail, with only ff. 107r and 108r overlapping the poem.
Public access and local research caching do not establish redistribution rights. Longleat's current request form limits supplied research images and requires separate written reproduction permission.

rights.component: British Library reference or reproduction images

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00095, a keyed encoding of Eleanor Prescott Hammond's complete 1911 Anglia article. Hammond directly examined and copied the anonymous Middle English translation from Longleat MS 258, ff. 102–119, and compared it with French texts printed by Thomas Wright and in Le Jardin de Plaisance. The anonymous English work translates Michault Taillevent's French Debat du coeur et de l'oeil of c. 1444 and also survives in an undated Wynkyn de Worde print conventionally dated c. 1516. Current evidence does not show that de Worde printed from Longleat: the manuscript's printer's marks do not match the surviving print and may indicate a lost second printing. Longleat reports that MS 258 was fully digitised, but only a selected public image set was found; whole-object access and reuse terms remain unresolved.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalogSummary.date: English translation made after the French work of c. 1444 and attested by c. 1475–1500; article published 1911
catalogSummary.region: Medieval England; no more precise place of translation is securely established
catalogSummary.title: The Eye and the Heart

catalogingSubject.label: The Eye and the Heart
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The complete thirty-one-page article published by Eleanor Prescott Hammond in 1911 and represented by CME00095: seven introductory prose paragraphs, Hammond's complete edition of the anonymous 824-line Middle English translation The Eye and the Heart, a manuscript-ending note, and a prose afterword. It is not only the medieval poem, the French source work, Longleat MS 258, the separate Wynkyn de Worde print, or the U-M encoding itself.

contentParts.label: Editorial introduction
contentParts.summary: Hammond describes Longleat MS 258, identifies the French source and early English print, surveys Eye-and-Heart allegories, and introduces the poem's chase, dream, tournament, and court-of-love motifs.

contentParts.label: The Eye and the Heart: Middle English poem

locators.value: ff. 102–119; Hammond's opening and ending notes use 102a and 119a

notes: The Longleat manuscript has no heading and begins with a four-line capital; the printed heading is editorial.
The public Longleat exhibition images overlap only two leaves within the complete f. 102–119 work portion.
contentParts.summary: The complete anonymous Middle English translation in 103 eight-line stanzas. A hunt leads into a dreamlike court where the personified Eye and Heart dispute responsibility for love, joust, and plead before Venus, who defers judgment to lovers.

contentParts.label: Manuscript-ending note
contentParts.summary: Hammond states that the poem ends on fol. 119a without an explicit; only one stanza occupies the page and the rest of the recto and the verso are blank.

contentParts.label: Editorial afterword
contentParts.summary: Hammond distinguishes padding introduced by expanding French octosyllables into longer English lines from errors attributable to the late Longleat copyist and assesses the manuscript's textual affiliations and limitations.

assertion.notes: The French work's approximate composition and the manuscript's approximate copying date provide termini, not a precise date for the English translation.

dateStatements.display: After the French work of c. 1444 and no later than the c. 1475–1500 Longleat copy

assertion.notes: The print is undated and is conventionally dated [1516?]; printer's marks in Longleat may point to a different, lost printing.

dateStatements.display: Possibly c. 1516 in Wynkyn de Worde's undated printing

extent.description: One complete thirty-one-page article (Anglia 34, pp. 235–265): seven introductory prose paragraphs; a complete 103-stanza, 824-line poem; a one-paragraph manuscript-ending note; and a one-paragraph afterword. XML counts are 3 DIV1, 103 LG, 824 L, 105 HEAD, 56 NOTE, 31 PB, 164 MILESTONE, and 10 P; the tenth P is the poem's ‘MS. Longleat 258’ headnote rather than an additional prose paragraph.

formStatements.description: Substantive English prose scholarship surrounds a complete Middle English verse edition.
103 eight-line stanzas, 824 lines, with rhyme pattern ababbcbc; Hammond says the English translator expands the French octosyllables toward ten-syllable lines.

notes: The cataloging record deliberately describes Hammond's complete 1911 article while separately exposing the medieval poem, French source, manuscript, and early-print layers.

The Longleat copy is the direct source of Hammond's English text. British Library Royal MS 19 A III and Le Jardin de Plaisance are French comparison witnesses, not established physical exemplars used by the anonymous translator.
The Longleat exhibition's statement that MS 258 was fully digitised does not mean that a complete facsimile is publicly available; the public selection overlaps only ff. 107r and 108r of the poem.

next_steps: Consult a modern critical edition and linguistic study of the English poem.
Keep French composition, English translation, and Longleat copying dates and places distinct.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous Middle English translation be dated or localized more narrowly than after the French work of c. 1444 and by the c. 1475–1500 Longleat witness?

next_steps: Compare the full surviving de Worde copy with every printer's mark in a complete MS 258 facsimile.
Do not connect Longleat directly to STC 6915 unless layout or bibliographic evidence establishes that copying relationship.
openQuestions.question: Which printing, if any, was prepared from the printer-marked Longleat manuscript?

next_steps: Request or obtain a complete Longleat facsimile and compare every italic span against manuscript abbreviation and letter forms.
Record a sign-by-sign expansion policy without treating italics as ordinary emphasis.
notes: Longleat's official access page and current image-request form provide the identified request and licensing route; no complete facsimile was consulted for this record.
openQuestions.question: Which italic letters in Hammond and CME are abbreviation expansions, editorial supplies, or other typographic signals in Longleat MS 258?

assertion.notes: This broad cultural-origin statement does not localize the place of translation or the copying place of Longleat MS 258.
Hammond compares the English poem directly with the French work; no translator is named.
Hammond says that Lord Bath allowed her to examine and copy Longleat MS 258.

subjects.label: Dispute between the Eye and the Heart
Manuscript description, translation comparison, and textual criticism

summaries.value: Hammond introduces Longleat MS 258 and the poem's French and early-print contexts, edits the complete 103-stanza Middle English love debate in which the Eye and Heart submit their quarrel to Venus, records the manuscript ending, and closes with a critical assessment of the translation and copyist.

CME00095 preserves the complete article rather than only the poem: printed pp. 235–237 are introduction, pp. 237–264 contain the poem and manuscript-ending note, and pp. 264–265 contain the afterword. The poem itself occupies Longleat MS 258, fols. 102–119 and has no manuscript heading or explicit.

notes: This is Hammond's article heading and therefore the preferred title for the complete cataloging subject.
Hammond supplied this heading in print; she explicitly says that the manuscript poem itself has no heading.

assertion.notes: Title normalized from the colophon title of the separate Wynkyn de Worde witness; it is not a Longleat manuscript rubric.

titles.value: A Lytel Treatyse called the Dysputacyon or Complaynt of the Heart thorughe perced with the Lokynge of the Eye
In the first weke of the saisoun of May

assertion.notes: The line ends the text; Hammond states that Longleat MS 258 has no written explicit.

titles.value: That to al his desires he may attayne
